prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 22-year-old Black man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I've had pain in my right lower belly since yesterday. It's a dull ache around 5/10. I feel a little nauseous but haven't vomited and I have less appetite. I had a stomach bug last week that's mostly better now.
- History: No urinary burning. No prior surgeries.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-belly pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but one important concern is appendicitis. Appendicitis often starts as a dull ache and can worsen over hours to a day; nausea and appetite loss are common. A recent stomach bug can sometimes mimic or precede other abdominal problems, so it doesn’t rule appendicitis out. Other possibilities include intestinal inflammation, constipation, or less commonly urinary or testicular issues, but the location and symptoms mean appendicitis needs to be considered because delaying care can lead to rupture and serious infection.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-belly pain with nausea and reduced appetite can come from several causes, including appendicitis, which often starts as a dull ache and can worsen over 1–2 days. A recent stomach bug can also cause lingering intestinal irritation, but it shouldn’t be assumed to be the cause when pain is localized to the right lower abdomen. Other possibilities include constipation, swollen lymph nodes in the abdomen, or less commonly urinary/kidney issues (even without burning). Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses, new right-lower-abdominal pain lasting a day with nausea/appetite loss should be checked promptly, especially if it worsens, you develop fever, vomiting, or increasing tenderness.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right lower belly pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but one important concern is appendicitis. Early appendicitis can start as a dull ache and may not yet include vomiting, fever, or severe tenderness. Recent “stomach bug” symptoms can overlap with or mask appendicitis, and waiting can be risky because an inflamed appendix can worsen or rupture. Other possibilities include intestinal inflammation, constipation, or less commonly urinary/kidney issues, but your lack of urinary burning doesn’t rule out serious causes. Because appendicitis is time-sensitive and can’t be confirmed without an exam (and often imaging/labs), this should be assessed urgently.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

Other possibilities include lingering intestinal irritation after your recent stomach bug, constipation/gas, or less commonly urinary or testicular issues, but your symptoms fit a pattern that needs prompt evaluation because appendicitis can worsen quickly and may require urgent treatment.

Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses, new right-lower belly pain lasting since yesterday with nausea and poor appetite should be checked promptly, especially if it worsens, becomes sharp, you develop fever, vomiting, or pain with walking/coughing.

A recent stomach bug doesn’t rule it out. Other possibilities include intestinal inflammation, constipation/gas, or less likely urinary/kidney issues (you don’t have burning). Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses, new right-lower-quadrant pain lasting since yesterday deserves prompt in-person evaluation, especially if it gets worse, you develop fever, vomiting, increasing tenderness, or pain with walking/coughing.

Other possibilities include lingering intestinal inflammation after your recent stomach bug, constipation, or less commonly urinary/kidney or testicular issues (even without burning). Because appendicitis can worsen quickly and may need urgent treatment, this shouldn’t be watched at home without medical evaluation.
